rows.Iwi: Ngāti Naho, Ngāti Tipa- Ngāti Pāoa
rows.Ahunga / Source: Cowan, 1987, pp. 226-228. Legends of the Māori, Vol 1. Kaikōrero: Tohikuri Iwi: Ngāti Naho, Ngāti Tipa-
Cowan, 1930, p. 215; Cowan 1987, p. 226. Kaikōrero: Ngākoma Ngāmane

rows.Description / Meaning: KOHUKOHUNUI: 688m located on the Hunua Range
rows.Iwi: Ngāti Pāoa, Ngāi Tai, Ngāti Whanaunga

rows.Description / Meaning: TE AROHA: 953m high. Known dwelling place of Patupaiarehe
A breeze that caresses the cheek. “He haurekereke ngā tamariki a Tāwhiri-mātea.”
Another name of a tohu mate for Te Whānau-a-Nuku. Thick fog gathering on top of Pokohinu.
There was a black cloud hanging over the mouth of the Mōtū River before the tragic drowning of 16 school children and two adults. A koroua was heard repeating, “He kino kai te haere!”
rows.Iwi: Te Whānau-a-Tūtawake, Te Whānau-a-Tūwahiawa, Ngāti Paeakau

rows.Description / Meaning: During a tangihanga, torrential rain fell, accompanied with galeforce winds. It was noted by the pakeke in the tipuna whare at the time that it was no ordinary storm. A continuous loud rapping sound hit the outside of the walls of the whare, going round and round all night.

A tohu mate for Mere Mahaki. When thick fog is seen spilling down from the Maraenui peak she knows someone has passed away from Rākaunui – Kāwhia Moana. When she observed the fog falling down she would say, “He mate tōku kai te kāinga.” Mere is the eldest sister of Te Ata, she is related to the Koroheke whānau from Ngāti Maniapoto, Ngāti Apakura.

rows.English / Scientific Name: WHARAWHARA Fog, whirlwind, thunder, lightning, torrential rain
rows.Description / Meaning: The tohu mate is only seen within the Omaio Bay, nowhere else. It starts off on a fine day with thick soupy fog gathering on top of Pokohinu Pt, which flops into the moana encircling the whole bay. It then reconnects with the mainland near Motunui. The fine day quickly starts to darken, the fog on the moana thickens up and then starts to move. Thunder and lightning cracks along the ridges behind Otūwhare Marae. Wind starts to blow, growing in intensity; a torrential downpour follows. It is said that the whenua is mourning the imminent passing
Spiritual rain passing across the hills like birds’ feathers – falling in phases.
Light rain falling out of clear blue sky during summer.

1. Te Kahautū Maxwell witnessed double lunar rainbows with his whanaunga and mentor Sir Monita Delamere, Emma’s tungane, when they travelled from Whitianga to Mt Eden Prison to exhume Mokomoko’s remains. Monita saw these as tohu aituā as the lunar rainbows were not fully formed. These rainbows stayed with them until they reached Te Kaokaoroa past Matata. Te Kahautū heard Monita conducting karakia most of the way to Tāmaki. 2. Roka shared how she had seen two vivid-looking lunar rainbows outside her window just after sunset. She said it was so unusual, as the colours of the rainbow were so intense. The Matata tragedy occurred not long after this in May 2005. 3. Lunar rainbows seen as a tohu wairua. When seen you know it’s a warning to be vigilant, to be careful.
rows.Iwi: Te Whānau-a-Maru, Te Whānau-a-Pararaki - Te Whānau- a-Apanui whānui
rows.Kaikōrero: Roka Paora Iwi: Te Whānau-a-Tūtawake, Te Whānau-a-Tūwahiawa, Ngāti Paeakau, Te Whānau-a-Apanui Takiwā: Whitianga

rows.Description / Meaning: A tohu mate for Te Whānau-a- Hikarukutai. Thick fog gathering on top of Tamakitekapua peak.

Is the name of the hill which runs westerly from Kakanui to Moaha behind Waiorore. It is there where the lightning strikes at Te Kaha. When the flash is seen upon that hill it is a sign of the death of a chief,and is known as a rua kōhā. Mere composed a waiata tangi for her husband Te Whatu-a- Rangahua. Here is the text from the lament: “Tērā te ūira hiko tapatahi ana rā runga o Tūāhu; ko te tohu o te mate i tukua atu nei”. “The lightning flashed once on the summit of Tūāhu; ‘twas the sign of him now departed”.
One should never go through the centre of the rainbow. Many would stop, then conduct karakia before travelling on when a rainbow was seen close by.
WHETŪ-O-TE-MARAMA Kā Momo Tohu: Tohu aituā –
rows.Ahunga / Source: Tau, 2008, p. 103 Kaikōrero: Maru-kai-tātea276 Iwi: Ngāi Tahu, Kāti Mamoe

rows.Description / Meaning: Kā Momo Tohu: Tohu aituā – Sign of misfortune. Kā Momo Tohu: Tohu aituā –

rows.Ahunga / Source: Beattie, 1994, p. 197 Iwi: Ngāi Tahu, Kāti Mamoe

rows.Description / Meaning: Extreme weather event, weather bomb. The top half of Te Tara-o-Te-Ika is susceptible to violent storms.
Heavy rainfall associated with an easterly

rows.Ahunga / Source: Ngā Mōteatea IV – Ngata, 2007, pp. 288-289. Waiata No.373: He waiata. Line No.1. Example: E ua rā e te ua, tātarahi ana koe i runga rā – Let it fall, oh rain, shower down from above. Kaitito / Kaikōrero: Powhiri

rows.Description / Meaning: When the kowhai flowers begin to fall, the September rains are expected. This is
Tempest, severe storm, roaring of the wind
Heavy dew forms during the evening on the grass
Very light misty conditions seen in the forest associated with tūrehu

Kōhai rains: When the kahawai fishing is finished at the mouth of the Mōtū, and their scales and blood are lying around on the beach, Te Whānau-a-Apanui elders would wait for `Marangai o te kōhai’ to cleanse the river mouth by washing away all the detritus.
Spiritual rain passing across the hills like birds’ feathers – falling in phases
Dew drops of the evening. Te Whānau-a-Apanui elders all mentioned that their mothers would say to them gather their clothes before the dew settles. Vapour in the air
Light drizzle, damp air. When it falls on your skin it quickly evaporates
Drizzling rain. Kai te kotiti te awhā
Easterly wind. If it blew for more than a week, rain would follow.
A strong northwest or southwest wind accompanied by rain

rows.Ahunga / Source: Ministry for Culture and Heritage, 2010, p.33

rows.Description / Meaning: South-west. Stormiest conditions bringing heavy rainfall, and gusts until it blows out. The south-west wind is known as toka when blowing ordinarily but when blowing a gale from this quarter it is known as `Puaitaha’.
rows.Takiwā: Waitaki, Otepoti, Moeraki, Tuahiwi, Rāpaki, Te Umukaha, Waikouaiti

rows.Description / Meaning: Billowing, gusting wind off the moana.
rows.Ahunga / Source: Wakareo. Hei tauira: Nā te apu tai koe i tukituki ki roto o Hauraki

rows.Description / Meaning: The whirlwinds of heaven. Sourced from Tīwai Paraone transcript. His classification of the different winds is similar to Ngāti Maniapoto.

rows.Iwi: Ngāti Tamaterā, Ngāti Whanaunga, Ngāti Pāoa. Rohe: Hauraki

rows.Description / Meaning: North-westerly wind. Usually a strong, blustery wind coming off the moana.

rows.Iwi: Ngāti Pare-Ngāti Tamaterā, Ngāti Maru, Ngāti Pāoa, Ngāti Whanaunga, Ngāti Pūkenga
rows.Ahunga / Source: Lionel Richards, John Tūhoe. Kaikōrero: Te Hiringānuku Ngāmane, Haumarangai Conner

rows.Description / Meaning: North wind The arid north wind
rows.Kaikōrero: Te Hiringānuku Ngāmane. Iwi: Ngāti Tamaterā, Marutūāhu whānui. Takiwā: Hauraki

rows.Ahunga / Source: Ngā Mōteatea IV – Ngata, 2005, pp. 82-83. Waiata: 322: He waiata tautitotito. Rārangi: 26. He Kōrero: Also known as Nini, Te Rangiānini and Tūterangiānini. He is a nephew to Tokoahu. Te Popo’s matua is Te Tūiri an ineffectual

rows.Description / Meaning: Easterly wind. Heavy rainfall associated with an easterly. Causes the Manāia Harbour to be stirred up. Possible flooding across the road.
rows.Ahunga / Source: Paraone, 1907, pp. 12, 16. Kaikōrero: Toko Renata Te Taniwha

Ngā Mōteatea IV – Ngata, 2007, pp. 94-95. Waiata: 325 He whakautu tautitotito. Rārangi: 3. Hei tauira: Riro te whakaaro ki ngā hau o te tonga, ripoa mai nei he ripo hauāuru – Reflective thoughts are gone with the south wind, Whirling about are these currents from the west. Kaitito / Kaikōrero: Tokoahu.185

rows.Description / Meaning: The Sanctified Heaven of Fragrant Breezes
rows.Iwi: Ngāti Tamaterā-Marutūāhu whānui. Rohe: Hauraki whānui

rows.Ahunga / Source: Maori Religion and Mythology: Shortland, 1882, p. 13

Ngā Mōteatea II – Ngata, 2005, pp. 58-59. Waiata.109: He waiata aroha. Rārangi: 8. Hei tauira: Ko te mātao rā, i tope i raro rā he wini tukunga mai nōu rā, e te hoa – A numbing cold I feel below from the breeze blowing hither, O my mate. Kaitito: Unknown

rows.Description / Meaning: Breeze. Not spoken about how strong or what direction it blows but more about a romantic reference, how it caresses the cheek or touchs the body, “Pā mai te hau-angiangi ki te pāpāringa.”
Westerly wind (W: typically hot dry winds)
North-easterly wind (NE: typically strong wind with rain)
Sea breeze, onshore wind. Locals used this breeze to get back to shore.
Strong, salt-laden sea-breeze. Also known as Hau-waho, prevailing westerly wind
Southerly wind (S). See Ngā Tohu Taiao

Wind of the sea (N: north wind). Also known as Hau-patu-kai. Prevailing wind (W: westerly). Due to the salt-laden wind, crop leaves like kūmara are burnt. Erect Pā- tūtū or Pā-koro. Starts to blow from 10am up until 5-6pm and then it stops
Wind that dried the crops. Also known as Hau-tōtoko. Wind causing salt-laden spray from the moana would burn the leaves of the crops. Screens of manuka called pā tūtū were erected to shelter them.
Blustery cirrus wind clouds that look like `white manes’ of a horse, formed by a southerly
Wind that causes rolling swells on the moana. “Ko te hau rā, kei te pupuhi mai i te wai kia haramai.”
Destructive easterly that comes down the valley and creates havoc.
South-east breeze. Mawake was also known as the tipuna who assisted in the netting of the kahawai at the Mōtū River.
Wind that blows along the coastline.

Zephyr on the sea (afternoon wind). Lighter than a squall but enough to cause a slight chop. Special wind, north-east wind. When this wind blew, the home people would rush down to the moana to take their boat out to fish. The Pāwhakarua would cause the tamure (snapper) to come to the surface. Broken kuku were used as bait. In this way a whole load of fish was caught in 10 minutes.
Rogue wind, very violent, causes boats to twist, jerk and spin, leading to extreme damage; masts are sometimes broken
Squally wind, causes a chop. If this is a northwest wind waka are able to get ashore a lot faster

rows.Ahunga / Source: Ancient History of the Maori - Vol II: White, 1887, p. 60 [Māori]
Ancient History of the Maori - Vol I: White, 1887, p. 92 [Māori], 105 [Pākehā]

rows.Description / Meaning: Southern gale - starting as a breeze then intensifying into a gale.
rows.Ahunga / Source: Ancient History of the Maori – Vol I; White, 1887, pp. 24, 28. See Puaitaha

rows.Description / Meaning: North-west wind - rough, boisterous winds Westerly
rows.Iwi: Ngāi Tahu Takiwā: Murihiku Ahuka: Beattie, 1994

rows.Description / Meaning: Northerly wind - a wind coming from between the north-east & north-west directions

rows.Ahunga / Source: Moorfield, 2005 Hai tauira: Ka rikoriko mai te mata o Puaka i te hauraro, he tohu tau pai - Rigel twinkles in the north, a sign of a good year
Beattie, 1994, p.359; Te Whare Hiko Rongo Kōrerotanga Māori Dictionary

rows.Description / Meaning: Sea breeze - this sea breeze rises on the 15th night of the moon
rows.Ahunga / Source: Ancient History of the Maori - Vol II: White, 1887, p. 11

rows.Description / Meaning: Inland wind - is a breath of wind from inland and as you get up in the morning you will feel it. It comes like a draught of cold air but is soon gone.
Land breeze - Te hau ka pupuhi mai i te tuawhenua.
rows.Ahunga / Source: New Zealand Māori Language Commission, 2008, p.95 Hai tauira: 1. He hau whakamaroke kai te hauwhenua i Ngā-Pākihi-Whakatekateka-a-Waitaha. 2. He hau māriri ka āta pupuhi mai i te tuawhenua. 3. Me kore ake te hauwhenua i āhua hauhau ai ngā kāinga o te mānia. 4. Rite tonu ngā kupu e whai ake nei – Pararaki, tāwhenua

rows.Description / Meaning: Controls the easterly and the northerly winds
West winds - when this breeze rises boisterous winds from other directions and rough seas are calmed West winds
rows.Ahunga / Source: JPS: Vol 26, No.3. Traditions and Legends – Collected from the Natives of Murihiku (Southland, New Zealand); Beatties, 1917, p.108

rows.Description / Meaning: West winds - when this breeze rises boisterous winds from other directions and rough seas are calmed
rows.Ahunga / Source: Ancient History of the Maori – Vol I: White, 1887-1890, pp. 24-28
Ancient History of the Maori – Vol 1: White, 1887, p.19

rows.Description / Meaning: Nor-westerly - This wind “knocks everybody around”. Taare mentioned that when he was on the boats if anyone noticed a nor- west wind they would immediately turn for home. The wind would inevitably swing around and start blowing from the south. The Kaihautangata or norwester was a notorious wind for causing many waka to capsize. It was difficult to get back into port when it was blowing from this quarter.
rows.Kaikōrero: Hana Morgan, Taare Bradshaw, Dean Whaanga, Tā Tipene O’Reagan

rows.Ahunga / Source: Ancient History of the Maori – Vol III: White, 1887, p. 49 [Pākehā], 29[Māori]. See Te Ope-rua-riki, Pūnui, Maroro-uri and Maroro-tea

rows.Description / Meaning: East wind - when this wind blew it was usually followed by a violent storm.

North-east wind - a hot wind West wind - warm wind Nor’wester wind - when a strong nor’wester blew, either wet or dry, the people would beat the swamps and drive the pakura to fly against the wind; after half an hour they got tired and dropped to be caught. Strong nor’wester - rough, boisterous winds North-west, west wind - cold wind Nor-wester Norwester
rows.Iwi: Ngāi Tahu Takiwā: Ōtautahi Ahuka: Beattie, 1994, p.360 Hapū: Kāti Huirapa ki Puketeraki, Kāti Wheke Takiwā: Waikouaiti, Rapaki Ahuka: Beattie, 1990, p.134
rows.Takiwā: Te Umukaha Ahuka: Beattie, 1994, p.359 Hai kōrero āpiti: Also known as Potiki tāne, see Tā. Rev J. Watkin. Ahuka: Beattie, 1994, p.197

rows.Description / Meaning: Easterly wind - if it kept blowing for a week it would be followed by rain.
Strong east- nor-easter - a strong wind coming out of this quarter.
rows.Ahunga / Source: Beattie, 1990, p.47; Beatties, 1994, p.360 Hai kōrero āpiti: A weak east nor-easter is called a Whakarua

Moorfield, 2005 Hai Tauira: He hau rongonui te parera o Waitaha, pērā anō hoki te hau tonga, ka kawe i ngā naku mai i Te Tiri o Te Moana (Te Ara 2012) - Canterbury’s nor’wester is a well known wind, as is the southerly, which blasts up from Antartica

rows.Description / Meaning: East-south-east - Light winds South-east wind of variable character South-east
rows.Iwi: Ngāi Tahu Takiwā: Otākou ki te tonga Ahuka: Beattie, 1994
rows.Takiwā: Rapaki Ahuka: Ngāi Tahu Tribal Dictionary

rows.Ahunga / Source: Vol 3: White, 1887, p. 49 [Pākehā], 29[Māori]. See Te Ope-rua-riki, Pūnui and Mara-kai-a-tinaku. Maori Religion and Mythology, Shortland, 1882, p. 13
Vol 3: White, 1887, p. 49 [Pākehā], 29[Māori]. See Te Ope-rua-riki, Pūnui and Mara-kai-a-tinaku. Maori Religion and Mythology, Shortland, 1882, p. 13

rows.Description / Meaning: North and South - group of winds that blew from the north and south.
rows.Ahunga / Source: Keane, 2012, p. 5. `Tāwhiri-mātea – the weather – Wind and storms’, Te Ara – the Encyclopedia of New Zealand. Hai kōrero āpiti: Within this group Te Pūnui o Te Toka was the southerly & Rakamaomao’s child known as Tiu was the northern wind

rows.Description / Meaning: Sou-wester wind - Potikitāne is a boiterous rough wind
Nor-wester wind - a boiterous rough wind
South-west gale force winds. Violent gale - when a `toka’ or a south west wind increases in strength to a gale it is called a pūaitaha; an exceptionally freezing cold gale. See Te Ope-ruariki
South-west wind - He hau ka pupuhi mai i te tonga-mā-uru.
rows.Ahunga / Source: New Zealand. Māori Language Commission, 2008, p.693 Hai tauira: Me kore ake te puāwanga i kawea ai ngā para ki rāwāhi. Rite tonu ki te kupu tonga
Vol 3: White, 1887, p. 49 [Pākehā], 29[Māori]. See Te Ope-rua-riki, Mara-kai-a- Tinaku, Maroro-uri and Maroro-tea

rows.Description / Meaning: South-south-east - associated with dirty, rainy weather. South-south-east wind
North-west wind - when a red- parrot fish is caught, considered a sign that a north-west wind will blow.
rows.Ahunga / Source: Andersen, 1907, p. 256 Hai kōrero āpiti: This north-west wind named after the red-parrot fish

rows.Description / Meaning: Westerly North-west wind - rough, boisterous winds South-west, southerly - hard, dry wind West wind Westerly
rows.Iwi: Kāti Mamoe, Ngāi Tahu Takiwā: Murihiku, Rakiura Ahuka: Henry James Fletcher: J.P.S. 65:169

rows.Description / Meaning: Storm, Tempest - offshore gale force winds
rows.Ahunga / Source: JPS: Vol 26, No.2; Traditions and Legends – Beatties, 1917, p. 82

rows.Description / Meaning: Nor’wester – forerunner of the North west arch. Most dominant wind in this rohe. Also known as Te Hau Kai Takata or The Wind that devours Humankind.
South-west wind - if it increases in strength to a gale, it is called a pūaitaha; an exceptionally freezing cold gale.
rows.Ahunga / Source: Ancient History of the Maori Vol 3. White, 1887, p.12 [Maori]; Beattie, 1994, p.197
Keane, 2012, p. 5; 2010, p.34. Hai kōrero āpiti: Within this group Te Pūnui o Te Toka was the southerly & Rakamaomao’s child known as Tiu was the northern wind
Vol 3; White, 1887, p. 51 [Pākehā], 31[Māori]. See Te Ope-rua-riki, Mara-kai-a- Tinaku, Maroro-uri and Maroro-tea

rows.Description / Meaning: The power of the wind The Origin or beginning of the Southerly Winds
Southerly direction - one of the stormiest winds bringing rain. Variable wind South-west wind - this south-west wind is known as toka when
rows.Takiwā: Lower Waitaki Plains, Otepoti, Moeraki, Southern Canterbury, Banks Peninsula
rows.Ahunga / Source: Beattie, 1994, pp.197-199, 361. Hapū: Ngāi Tūahuriri, Kāti Wheke, Kāti Huirapa ki Arowhenua

rows.Description / Meaning: Sou-westerly wind - when it starts to blow it builds and builds and then brings torrential rain from that quarter. Sou-westers bring the most extreme weather.

Land breeze - this breeze was exceptionally cold when it came off the mountains and along the Waitaki valley. When it blew Otautahi ki te toka was much colder than Murihiku. A disagreeable wind. Land breeze that blows in the morning.
rows.Ahunga / Source: Beattie, 1994, p. 200. Hai kōrero āpiti: Any breeze that came off a river valley was known as a tūwhenua

Beattie, 1994, p. 198. Hai kōrero āpiti: Also known to come from north-north-east. Famous north-west wind that blows across the Canterbury Plains sometimes creating wind shear. Warm, turbulent wind. When the nor-wester finishes blowing, a southerly follows. Hai tauira: Kai te taki te wahanui ki te toka, kai te tono atu ki te toka tō taki - The cry of
Beattie, 1994, p. 197. Hai kōrero āpiti: Known as Potiki wahine or fine winds. The people say that when the Waihola & other easterly winds blew they were `nice, gentle winds’, mild sometimes, coldish

rows.Description / Meaning: Southerly - wind direction coming from Lake Waihora (Ellesmere) Sou-easter - inclement weather wet and cold associated with this wind that swirled around for three days.
rows.Ahunga / Source: Beattie, 1994, p. 360. Hapū: Kāti Huirapa ki Arowhenua Takiwā: Te Umukaha Kaikōrero: Mandy Waaka-Home

rows.Description / Meaning: Westerly, land breeze, northerly - a Kaiapoi elder mentioned that the northerly wind was known as wawa.
North-east, easterly wind - strong and boisterous East-north-east wind - sometimes whakarua was a wet or dry wind East wind Weak east-nor’easter
rows.Hapū: Kāti Huirapa ki Arowhenua Takiwā: Te Umukaha Ahuka: Beattie, 1994, p. 199. Iwi: Ngāi Tahu Takiwā: Murihiku, Rakiura Ahuka: Henry James Fletcher: J.P.S. 23:78; 25:111; 53:211; 65:169; 108:270. W.W. 2:147, 157. Iwi: Ngāi Tahu

rows.Description / Meaning: The shapes of clouds can show from what direction wind is expected. If the cloud was pointed wind would blow from that quarter.
Known also as `Strips in the sky’ or `Mares’ tails’, very high, wispy clouds. Changing weather pattern within 2-3 days, usually from fine weather to bad.
rows.Iwi: Ngāti Tamaterā, Marutūāhu whānui. Rohe: Hauraki whānui
Ngāti Kiriwera, Ngāti Taharua, Ngāti Taiuru-Ngāti Tamaterā

rows.Description / Meaning: Dark, billowing storm clouds. Kapua-pango type clouds can produce torrential rain and can cause flash flooding, accompanied by destructive, damaging winds. Most kapua-pango storm clouds die away after about 20 minutes, due to the heavy rainfall.
rows.Iwi: Ngāti Kiriwera, Ngāti Taharua, Ngāti Taiuru-Ngāti Tamaterā
Ngāti Kiriwera, Ngāti Taharua, Ngāti Taiuru-Ngāti Tamaterā

rows.Description / Meaning: A lot thicker than mist. When tendrils of mist and fog descend from the mountain tops, kaumatua would say either Patupaiarehe had come to get you or our tupuna were amongst us.
rows.Iwi: Ngāti Kiriwera, Ngāti Taharua, Ngāti Taiuru-Ngāti Tamaterā

rows.Description / Meaning: Saucer-like clouds positioned over mountains. Usually indicated that rain was expected. Mother would always look towards Te Aroha to check what the weather would be like for

Very light fog. `Ngā puke ki Hauraki, ka tarehu’, `The hills of Hauraki are covered in mist’. Hauraki is well known for its mountain ranges being the haunt of tūrehu and patupaiarehe. They are nearly always covered in mist.
rows.Iwi: Ngāti Kiriwera, Ngāti Taharua, Ngāti Taiuru-Ngāti Tamaterā

rows.Description / Meaning: Thick, dark billowing clouds. These threatening dark clouds indicated that a violent storm was imminent. When Ngāti Pare saw this they went to high ground, moving away from any waterways due to possible flash flooding.
`The net of Taramainuku’. Low-lying overcast clouds that looked like the scales of a fish. Usually means it is either becoming overcast or the weather is clearing.
`The Kūmara gardens of Ngātoro-i-rangi’. Māori saw this type of cloud as resembling a raised bed of kūmara. High level clouds appearing as a group of small masses of white or pale blue,

rows.Ahunga / Source: Ngā Mōteatea II – Ngata, 2005, pp. 50-51 Waiata No.106: He waiata aroha Rārangi No.16 Hai kōrero āpiti: Synonymous with Aorewa Kōrero: Unknown

rows.Description / Meaning: High wind, streaky clouds across the sky. Clouds like the `white manes’ of a horse formed by a southerly coming over the Raukūmara Ranges. Blustery wind, building up to storm conditions, “he kino kei te hara mai.”

rows.Ahunga / Source: Ngā Mōteatea II – Ngata, 2005, pp. 50-51 Waiata No.106: He waiata aroha Rārangi No.16 Kōrero: Unknown

rows.Description / Meaning: Cloud forming a thin layer at high altitudes, could be a precursor to rain.
White wispy clouds at high altitudes, mist lying in small detached portions
rows.Ahunga / Source: Ngā Mōteatea II – Ngata, 2005, pp. 50-51 Waiata No.106: He waiata aroha Rārangi No.16 Hai kōrero āpiti: Synonymous with Aorewa Kōrero: Unknown

rows.Description / Meaning: Thick clouds in rounded white masses
rows.Ahunga / Source: Ngā Mōteatea II – Ngata, 2005, pp. 50-51 Waiata No.106: He waiata aroha Rārangi No.16 Kōrero: Unknown
Ngā Mōteatea II – Ngata, 2005, pp. 50-51 Waiata No.106: He waiata aroha Rārangi No.16 Hai kōrero āpiti: Synonymous with Aorewa Kōrero: Unknown

rows.Description / Meaning: `Mares tail’. High cirrus clouds. Rain expected.
Cirrus cloud – Mare’s tail. Very high wispy clouds

rows.Ahunga / Source: The Ancient History of the Maori, Vol 1. White, 1887, p. 140

rows.Description / Meaning: Dark cloud – associated with light rain
Heavy clouds - Masses of thick, heavy clouds. Cumulonimbus clouds. When these clouds are seen an intense storm is imminent
Cloud lying on a mountain – associated with snow
rows.Ahunga / Source: Te Reo Tupu: rd – 44565/194048. Hai tauira: Tērā te hukarere e tāhere i Koinaki (S.ii, 40). Te ao ka tāhere ki Timaru rā ia (S.ii, 70)

rows.Description / Meaning: Very dark, billowing, intimidating cloud, bunched together in rounded masses above mountains. Turbulent at cloud level. Violent storm imminent.
rows.Ahunga / Source: Moorfield, J. C. (2005). Te Aka: Māori-English, English-Māori Dictionary and Index. Longman. Hai tauira: I tētahi pō ka titiro ia ki te pō tū i waho i Te Omanga e tāruru ana, ki Te Ika o te Rangi me Ngā Pātari, ki te tae pūkohu tātaiore e taipua ana i ngā maunga (JPS, 1911, p. 17) - One night he looked at the clouds beyond Te Omanga, resting close and compact, at the Milky Way and the Magellan Clouds, at the misty clouds settling in masses on the mountains
Williams Dictionary, p.416. E tihore ana te pō, he hukapapa – kua tihore te rangi

rows.Description / Meaning: Stratocumulus clouds - thick, dark clouds. Usually produces no rain, but if it does it is only light. Usually seen before a storm front or after one.
Dark clouds that lead to continuous rainfall

rows.Indicator (Tohu): South-easterly starts blowing
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Winds build in strength. Storm imminent.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Kākā seen acting up squawking, twisting and turning above the ridge line or the canopy of the forest.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 6-8 hours later violent storm strikes the northern tip of Te Tara-o-te-Ika. This storm will hang around for at least three days.

rows.English / Scientific Name: Located on the west side of Hapuakohe Ranges
rows.Indicator (Tohu): Dark clouds gather upon the summit of Maungaroa
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Rainfall imminent
rows.Iwi: Ngāti Naho, Ngāti Hine, Ngāti Pāoa
rows.Ahunga / Source: Cowan, 1987, pp. 226-228. Legends of the Māori, Vol 1

rows.Indicator (Tohu): `Island sinking out of sight’. In certain weather conditions Motukoreha seems to disappear or seem to be suspended above the moana when viewed from a distance.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Bad weather was expected from the east. These mirage- like conditions signalled to local Māori that wind was also expected.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Compressed air created by heavy surf causes a moaning sound like a dog.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Bad storm approaching.

rows.English / Scientific Name: `Place of driftwood’. Located at Birkdale Landing at the end of Beach Haven Road.
rows.Indicator (Tohu): Driftwood seen along the beach at Opaketai
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Predominantly strong westerly winds expected.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. The changing cries of the ‘ruru’. 2. The cries of two or more ‘ruru’ can be heard and typically their calls are shriller.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Warning of approaching rainfall. 2. Inclement weather

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Potae (Lenticular cloud) over Te Aroha
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Rainfall imminent approximately 24 hours

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Potae (Lenticular cloud) over Moehau
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Rainfall imminent approximately 24 hours

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Mist on the peaks of Rangitoto. 2. Clear skies above Rangitoto
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Rain expected 2. Fine weather expected.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): The west and eastern ends of Whakaari observed lifting out of the water and the island also looks a lot closer
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Wind expected from that quarter

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Halo around the moon
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Bad, windy weather ahead
rows.Hapū: Te Whānau-a- Hikarukutai, Te Whānau-a- Kauaetangohia

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Booming, roaring sound in the Kereu catchment
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Rising floodwaters expected. Go to higher ground.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Dust-like appearance to the north, on the horizon out to sea. “Kai te puehu te pae, he hau tonga kai te haere”
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): A violent, blustery southerly strikes the Te Whānau-a-Apanui coastline in 3-4 days

rows.Indicator (Tohu): High wind, streaky clouds across the sky. Clouds like the `white manes’ of a horse formed by a southerly.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Blustery wind, building up to storm conditions, “he kino kei te hara mai.”

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Severe frosts
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Fine weather. Frost on the land, fine weather expected. Easy to catch fish during these conditions. Once the frosts disappear, the moana is rough the next day.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Mist gathering near the mouth of rivers and
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Southerly on its way

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Mist gathering near the mouth of river and as it travels along the beach it fizzles out half way along it. 2. Mist gathering near the mouth of river and travels along the beach and reaches Arthur’s house.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. The conditions of the moana are safe for fishing. 2. The conditions of the moana are not safe for fishing.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): North-north-west wind.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): This wind is usually accompanied by rain.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Halo around the sun
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Rainstorm ahead

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Lifting of the Manuka canopy by wind off the sea.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Storm approaching.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Torrential rainfall in the upper catchment of Te Waitī/Pūangiangi over 2- 3 days
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Flooding expected along the Papatea flood plains near Raukokore.
rows.Kaikōrero: Huhana Wright, Ripeka Martin, Tony Wattam, Noki Martin, Arama Koopu

rows.Indicator (Tohu): The booming sound of breaking waves across the beach along the Omaio (Tokamaia) Bay and the particular sound created when the waves struck certain rocks during a fine day. Tokatā is another rock that makes a certain noise.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Storm approaching next day.
rows.Hapū: Te Whānau-a-Rūtaia, Te Whānau-a-Hikiarukutai, Te Whānau-a-Kauaetangohia
rows.Kaikōrero: Wīremu Tāwhai, Danie Poihipi, Tahanga Kemara

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Pāua’s muscular foot attached to a rock, and the shell extended upright to trap seaweed
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Storm approaching

rows.Indicator (Tohu): A pale ring - halo around the sun.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Bad weather is expected soon, within 6 – 12 hours.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Clouds like a potae over Tihirau during a fine clear day.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Heavy rain storm approaching.
rows.Hapū: Te Whānau-a- Kauaetangohia, Te Whānau-a- Tapuaeururangi

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Two coastal rocks (one still known called Tokatā) near Wakatiri (Lottin Point) make a noise on a fine day.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Rough weather expected the next day.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. The plume lies to the left. 2. The plume rises upwards intact across the horizon. 3. The plume rises straight up and bends to the east 4. The plume rises upwards, flattens and then breaks off.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Rainfall is expected. 2. Continued fine weather. 3. A high, light westerly breeze256 4. Watch out! Get off the ocean. Violent storm approaching.

rows.English / Scientific Name: RAUTUTU North west wind - offshore breeze
rows.Indicator (Tohu): A squally wind. This indicator follows the manuka lifting due to a squally off-shore breeze.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): This is an indicator to fishermen that the moana will start to `chop up’ shortly. If this is a north west

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Haze viewed early morning (5am) about tree height
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Extremely hot conditions that day

rows.Indicator (Tohu): A light colour in the sky to the west, inland above Canterbury Plains viewed from Banks Peninsula.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Fine weather expected

rows.Indicator (Tohu): A pale bluish light out to sea due south
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Forewarning of bad, southerly weather

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Autahi twinkles very clearly on one side and the other side is dimmer 2. If it is flashing on the southern side, a gale is imminent
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Blows hardest on the side which the star is flashing brightest

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Rivers that come off the Southern Alps change from a clear colour to a whitish-brown known as `waiarahi’ 2. The colouration of the river changes to a browny- orange, known as `waitohi’.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Alert sounded 2. Danger imminent – travelling party moved to sheltered, higher ground to await the storm to abate.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): The Hākoakoa makes a great noise while in its burrows
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): A storm is approaching. No man hearing this will venture out to sea on his waka.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): High clouds similar to horses’ tails observed above the Canterbury Plains
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): A shift in weather conditions from fine to bad, due in 2-3 days

rows.Māori Name: KĀ HIHI KANAPA O TE RĀ
rows.Indicator (Tohu): The sun rising above Hikuika (Mt Sinclair) preceded by:
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Rain imminent

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Red streaks around the peak 2. Grey streaks around the peak
Enormous ocean swells reach the west coast
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 12 hours later the first

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Aurora australis (southern lights) is clearly seen at night to the south.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Improving, fine weather next day approximately 6-8 hours.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Lightning flashing on the horizon
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): The side on which the flashes are the strongest signals the direction of the wind to come

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Very distinctive harsh screeching cry
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Rain imminent in 3-4 hours

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. A kārewarewa flies about, screaming on a fine day 2. A kārewarewa flies about, screaming on a rainy day
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. The next day rain is expected 2. The next day will be fine

rows.Indicator (Tohu): White caps appear within the Bluff Harbour
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Westerly (wawa) blowing. Stronger winds expected bringing rainfall from that quarter.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Fog suspended on top of the Tari-a-Te- Kaumira Range (Mt Nimrod) near Waimate and then descending downwards Fog suspended on top of Te Whiwhi hill 1. Fog descending down in a tongue shape from Oteoka Hill 2. Fog spread out and descended level
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Rain imminent Rain imminent 1. South west wind accompanied with rain expected 2. Fine weather followed

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Kōparapara heard screeching in the morning.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Rainfall imminent.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): The leaves295 seen turning upwards exposing the silvery underneath of the leaf
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Heavy downpour expected, a marakai
rows.Takiwā: Makāwhio, Te Tai Poutini Helen Rasmussen
rows.Ahuka Whakaahua: Tony Wills – Wikimedia Commons Iwi: Ngāi Tahu

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Repeated one cry through the night 2. Two or more answering each other for a certain time during the night
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Bad weather expected 2. A signal that storms are on their way

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Paired Kūaka entering a burrow
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Fine weather is expected the next day.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Calm sea conditions 2. Clouds tracking north 3. Roaring sea from the north
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Bad weather imminent

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Mohua suddenly rises above the forest canopy and then falls back amongst the trees
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Storm about to strike

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Swarms of namunamu start biting aggresively
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Rainfall is expected

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Naonao flying around at dusk
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Fine weather expected

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Easterly blowing for more than a week
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Rainfall follows

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Kelp weather-glass is full of air and hard to the touch 2. Kelp weather-glass is spongy and soft to the touch
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Fine weather ahead 2. Rain is imminent

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Parara arrive on the tītī mutton-birding Islands during the night
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Gales and heavy rainfall expected the next day.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. The grass is wet 2. The grass is dry
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. North east wind will blow 2. A southerly wind will blow

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Pīwaiwaka catching insects by the river
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Rain imminent

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Smoke plume forced downwards draft by a sudden drop in pressure
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Rainfall imminent

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Silence of the night is broken when Brown Tree Frogs are heard whistling and croaking
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Rainfall the next day.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Puaka appears south of the sunrise 2. Puaka appears north of the sunrise 3. Puaka appears in the path of the sun
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Bad weather is expected 2. Fine weather is expected 3. Much heat and dryness is expected

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Kelp is flaccid 2. Kelp is turgid
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Fine weather ahead 2. Wet weather ahead

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Red sky all around the horizon at dusk
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Fine weather expected

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Hazy, towering
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. High possibility of snowfall (4 hours later) on the Southern Alps.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Ghost moths observed flying near a light source.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Light, misty rain expected the next day

rows.Indicator (Tohu): A thin, `collar-like’ cloud surrounds Tarahaoa (Mt Peel)
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Rain imminent within three days.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Te Ika-a-Raki is curved 2. Te Ika-a-Raki is straight
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Bad weather expected 2. Fine weather expected

rows.Indicator (Tohu): A mass of dark clouds begin to form above the Southern Alps. Blue sky is seen above and below these clouds.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): A southerly is expected the next day. The strength of the southerly depends on the height of the arch above the mountains.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Tieke fly to the protected western side of the island or the east wind perch. Ahuka whakaahua: David Reneke – NZ Bird on line
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Wind from the east imminent.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Rain with no wind before a southerly 2. Rain accompanied with wind before a southerly
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Southerly will hang around Kaikoura for 2-3 days 2. Southerly will pass through Kaikoura quickly

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Ringing sound in the ear drum
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Warning that rain was imminent

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Hector’s Dolphins are unusually excited in the wake of boats during summer
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Storm on its way, driven by a northerly

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Lunar halo
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Sign of wind. Sometimes indicated fine weather and when a complete halo, heavy fog

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Vivid solar halo encircles the sun 2. A pale, dim halo encircles the sun
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. A storm is approaching 2. A storm is far off

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Wahanui starts to blow
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Flattens the sea off Te Umukaha coast, allowing fishing and diving to occur

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Rising level of the Kapuka Creek
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Rain imminent

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Aua caught near the confluence of the Waihou and Ōhinemuri Rivers
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Warmer spring and summer months are on the way.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Seen migrating downstream towards Tīkapa to breed.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): The colder months of late autumn, early winter were upon them.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): The cry of the koekoeā
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Heralds the commencement of spring

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Flowers of the kōwhai start to fall to the ground
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Kōwhai rains expected shortly during the month of September

rows.Indicator (Tohu): The cry of the kūaka
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Heralds the commencement of spring

rows.Indicator (Tohu): The cry of the pīpīwharauroa
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Heralds the commencement of spring

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Early prolific flowering from the bottom upwards. 2. Early prolific flowering from the top downwards.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Dry summer, drought expected. 2. Wetter than normal summer expected.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Early flowering
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Long, dry summer

rows.English / Scientific Name: Pteradroma macroptera gouldi - Grey Faced Petrels
rows.Indicator (Tohu): Oi seen gathering at the Ruamāhua Islands off the east coast of Te Tara-o- Te-Ika
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Warm, summer months are on the way.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Puna flowed excessively during spring weather. 2. Puna flowered weakly during spring weather.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Fruitful, abundant season expected. 2. Bad season expected.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Rivers are flooded, a lot of surface flooding. The head of the tuna becomes much more slender and tapered, almost
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Tuna puhi seen migrating on the outgoing tide to spawn, signalling to locals the commencement
rows.Iwi: Ngāti Pāoa, Ngāti Hako, Ngāti Tamaterā, Ngāti Tara, Tokanui, Tāwhaki

rows.Indicator (Tohu): The arrival of the kūaka.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Warmer season commences.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Manu Kāhawai nesting lower than usual at Puketapu or at Awanui.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Bountiful summer expected

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. In the first five nights of the lunar month, the crescent moon is lying on its back. 2. The crescent moon is standing up and down.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. A dry month lies ahead. “Kua apowai te marama.” Fine weather. 2. A wet month lies ahead. “Kua riringiwai te marama.” The

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. The shimmer around Pareārau is light and misty 2. Pareārau is clearly seen in the night sky.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Wet, cold conditions for the next month. 2. Dry month ahead.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Ducks moving their nests to higher ground.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Wetter than normal summer season ahead

rows.Indicator (Tohu): The arrival of the Pipiwharauroa.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Spring has arrived.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Periodic blooming.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): A warm season lies ahead with gentle breezes.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Flowering starts from the top branches & progresses downwards. 2. Flowering starts from the lower branches & progresses upwards.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Cold & wet summer will follow. 2. A long & hot (drought) summer will follow

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Autahi is seen standing far apart from the milky way during the month of October
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Autahi is seen standing

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Early flowering of the koladi stem (korari)
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Long, dry summer is expected

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. A mixture of blooms sticking up and down. 2. All blooms drooping. 3. All blooms erect.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Indicates a mixed year ahead. 2. Indicates a cooler year ahead. 3. Indicates a warmer year ahead.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Sea fog rolling in from the east along the Whakaraupō Harbour towards Rāpaki 2. Numerous sea fog events take place
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Cold, wintry

rows.Indicator (Tohu): The kōtuku are plentiful in summer along the West Coast at Ōkārito
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Gales and a heavy winter will follow

rows.Indicator (Tohu): The leaves of the Kōtukutuku are seen dropping to the ground.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Winter is on its way.
rows.Ahuka Whakaahua: Tony Willis – Wikimedia Commons Iwi: Ngāi Tahu

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Fine weather during the new moon phase 2. Wet weather during the new moon phase
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Fine weather

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Cresent moon seen lying on its back
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Drier conditons expected for that month

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. In June, Puaka rises, twinkling and flashing its rays towards the south
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. An unsettled, windy, cooler and lean year ahead
rows.Ahunga / Source: Te Karaka, 2000, p. 28; Cowan, 1930, p. 89; Beattie, 1994, p. 363

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 2. Puaka rises, twinkling and flashing its rays towards the north
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 2. A year of bounty on land and sea, and settled, warmer conditions ahead

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Pūtakitaki nesting near the river
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Dry summer season ahead

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Wet weather during a full moon lunar phase
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Wet month expected, it will be more wet than not

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Profuse flowering of the rātā from the bottom upwards
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Very dry, hot summer, drought-like conditions

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. The Riroriro builds a nest high in the tree- tops 2. The Riroriro chooses to build a home low down among sheltering branches
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Mild westerly winds will ensure a warm summer 2. Cold southerlies expected

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Profuse early flowering 2. Late flowering
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Long, dry summer ahead. 2. Wet summer expected

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Autahi seen rising in the east
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Signal that the Moki season is near, the moki have returned to Te Whānau-a-Apanui.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): The karaka tree is ablaze in bright fruit
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Kahawai returns to spawn during summer season. I ngā raumati, kua ura te karaka, kua hokihoki mai ngā kahawai ki te whakawhānau mai hēki. I ngā rā o mua ,

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Kōhai are flowering “Ka pūāwai te kōhai, ka tere te mārearea.”
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): The mārearea (whitebait) are running

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Manu Kahawai nesting closer to the rivers or estuaries of the Hāparapara.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Bountiful summer expected

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Zephyr, nor-east wind blowing
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Tamure are easily caught when a pāwhakarua blows

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Pōhutukawa flowering “Ka pūāwai te pōhutukawa, ka pātero te kina.”
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Kina is fat – summer is here

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Pūanga seen rising in the east
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Signal that the Moki season is near, the moki have returned to Te Whānau-a-Apanui.
rows.Kaikōrero: Huhana Wright, Ripeka Martin, Tony Wattam, Noki Martin, Arama Koopu

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Rarauhe has turned brown
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Kahawai run returns to spawn during summer season

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Frosty morning
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Sea conditions are excellent for fishing during winter

rows.Indicator (Tohu): The rising of Whānui bright and low at night approximately 9.30pm seen about mid-June.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Signal that the moki is migrating and therefore the moki season.

rows.Indicator (Tohu): HAO
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): The day is rough with a south-west wind and the night is dark (hinapouri).

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Kanakana come up the Opihi River (inland of Te Umukaha) to spawn during the rising of Puaka (Rigel). 2. Kanakana come up the Mataura River to spawn at night during a new moon when it is dark. Heavy rainfall.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): 1. Beginning of the new year in June 2. Kanakana caught in great numbers near the Mataura Falls from October to December

rows.Indicator (Tohu): 1. Male kiekie produces succulent, fleshy bracts called wharawhara. 2. Female kiekie produces 3-4 fruit called tāureure.
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): The fruit ripened during the month of May - Kāhuru

rows.Indicator (Tohu): The cry of the koekoeā
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Heralds the commencement of Summer

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Tui and kereru seen feeding on the berries
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): A sign that kōtukutuku berries known simply as hua kōtukutuku or konini are ready to be harvested in January

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Weka and kiore seen eating pātōtara298 during summer
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Indicator to local Māori that `Kā rā o te waru’ or the dry months of summer has arrived and that pātōtara are ready to eat. Produces a small yellow, orange, edible berry. Dies away in Winter

rows.Indicator (Tohu): The cry of the Pipiwharauroa
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Heralds the commencement of Spring

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Early flowering of Pūawānaka
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Excellent white baiting season expected Signals the approach of spring

rows.Indicator (Tohu): From December to March the feathers of the Pūtakitaki moult
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Pūtakitaki were chased

rows.Indicator (Tohu): The star Rehua seen in the night sky
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Signals the commencement of summer

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Inaka start to run up the Orari, Rakitata and Omihi Rivers during early September
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Signals to Kāti Huirapa ki Arowhenua to start processing the tī roots known as kāuru. This continues until March

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Arrival in Murihiku
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Heralds the commencement of summer

rows.Indicator (Tohu): Arrival of Tītī to Te Taurapa-o-Te Waka
rows.Expected Outcome (Whakataunga): Signals to the locals that harvesting of Weka can start from April to July.

glossary.Definition / English Translation: A term coined for a place that had been left behind by Polynesian migrants
Goddess of the mist and fog
Communal gatherings usually held at the marae
A feast whereby tangata whenua can express manaakitanga for their visitors and thus return them from a state of tapu to a state of noa
The Māori tribal nations of Aotearoa each descended from an eponymous ancestor of whom the iwi is sometimes named after
Rainbow God (Ngāi Tahu). The upper band of the rainbow is male called Kahukura-pango. The lower band is called Rokomai

Prayers or incantations addressed to the gods, and are offered so that the gods may intercede in the affairs of mortal beings by providing comfort, guidance, direction and blessings for them in their various activities and pursuits
Tribal elders who speak on behalf of their hapū and iwi
Self-determination, cultural aspiration, culturally preferred pedagogy, socio-economic mediation, extended family structure, and collective philosophy
(Freycinetia banksii) Native climber. Edible fruit and the leaves used for weaving
Home or describing the homes of tangata whenua

Mana is central to the relationships between individuals or collective groups or both; tangata whenua are responsible for ensuring that the mana of visitors is maintained; whānau members, young and old, are responsible for upholding the mana of their whānau

Is to care for a person's mana. Manaakitanga, therefore, means to care for a person's well-being in a holistic sense - that is physically, mentally, spiritually and psychologically. The mana of the host rises or falls on their ability to be generous hosts to their guests
An intimate interconnected relationship with the land

The open area in front of the wharenui (main meeting house), where formal greetings and discussions take place. The term is also used to describe the communal facilities which are symbols of whānau and hapū identity
Area directly in front of a traditional meeting house, a sacred place
Sacred mountains. Iwi identifies with a specific mountain that has special significance to them. Tribal pride, mana, and authority are synonymous with these mountains
Usually two bolts of lightning striking the summit of a sacred mountain - a sign of impending death of a tribal leader
Life force, life essence of all things animate and inanimate
The coastal waters of iwi and hapū

Shellfish, seafood. It is also an area of traditional fishing grounds which may encompass more than one. A spatial closure for the purposes of helping recognise use and management practices of iwi and hapū in the exercise of non-commercial fishing rights
'Eight sacred strands', a conceptual framework that I have developed to better understand MEK
Free from tapu or any other restriction. Profane, ordinary, without restraint, or denoting an absence of limitations or conditions
Physical representation of the oho - anything that has been associated with the person for whom the karakia is intended, e.g. a lock of hair.
Easterly blowing more than a week
Battlefields, sites where blood was spilt during battle
Tribal saying that connects an individual to a sacred mountain, river, sea, canoe, eponymous ancestor, and a tribe
Traditional food gathering sites. It also refers to storage houses for seasonal food which is harvested when in plentiful supply. See mātaitai.
Ritual encounter during a traditional welcome
Sovereignty, chieftainship, right to exercise authority, chiefly autonomy, chiefly authority, ownership, leadership of a social group, domain of the rangatira, noble birth, attributes of a chief
Underworld, dwelling place of the dead
Lesser deity of the rainbow god (Ngāi Tahu)
Rainbow Guardian. This god resides on the moon. The gods in this family are guardians of the rainbow bridge that leads through the heavens
Usually two bolts of lightning striking the summit of a sacred mountain - a sign of impending death of a tribal leader
Female seer, eldest female from a family of rank

A form of tapu restricting the use of land, sea, rivers, forests or other natural resources. It can be used to restrict entry, replenish resources or allow the tapu associated with death to dispense naturally by the natural elements of the air, sun, and rain
The mourning ceremonies and rituals preceding and including a funeral

Is a sacred condition of restriction, affecting persons, places, and things, and arising from innumerable causes. People need to be cautious that their actions do not affect the tapu of others. Similarly, people need to be careful that others do not affect their tapu
Traditional fishing grounds for finfish species

Places where ancestral waka (canoes) landed after long voyages, and also refers to traditional places where waka were brought ashore after returning from a voyage. Ideal canoe landing sites are flat so that minimal stress is placed on the hull of the canoe
Best practice, rules, customs, reasons, and meanings
Elder brother of a brother, or elder sister of a sister
Son of Tama-nui-te-rā, mirage, shimmering heat waves
The act of dedication; consisted of offering up a person, place or thing to the service of a particular atua
Either a lunar halo or a solar halo
A special hangi dedicated to the gods
Spirit associated with all animate and inanimate objects
Genealogy. The ability to know one's whakapapa and how iwi and hapū are connected by blood to other iwi and hapū is critical
Expression of kinship, and bringing people together through close family associations
Expression of kinship, and bringing people together through close family associations
The land. Māori are inextricably linked to the whenua. They view the whenua as their mother that provides the sustenance that they need to survive

Places which are sacred to tangata whenua, and may include burial sites, grounds where battles were fought, or sites of a highly spiritual nature which may be associated with healing, childbirth or other special purposes
The practice of traditional esoteric or higher learning
